name: Sorting and Counting — Our First Picture Chart
whatIsIt: When we have lots of things, we SORT them into groups (by colour, type or kind), COUNT each group, and show the counts as a simple picture chart — one picture for each thing. Then a quick look tells us which group has the most and which has the least.
whyNeeded: Sorting and counting turn a messy pile into a clear story. Which fruit is most liked? How many red buttons? Picture charts answer these at a glance, and they are the very first step towards graphs.
realLifeUses: Sorting toys into cars, blocks and dolls, then counting each
A class chart of favourite colours
Counting how many of each fruit are in a basket
Sorting the laundry into shirts, socks and towels

vocabulary.meaning: Put things into groups that are alike.
Find how many are in each group.
One picture for each thing, so groups can be compared.

story.title: Aarav Sorts the Fruit Basket
story.text: Amma gives Aarav a mixed basket: apples, bananas and mangoes all jumbled. 'Make it neat,' she says. Aarav sorts them into three piles and counts: 3 apples, 5 bananas, 2 mangoes. He draws one picture for each fruit in rows. 'Bananas have the most — the banana row is the longest!' he says proudly.
story.extractedProblem: Aarav sorts the basket: 3 apples, 5 bananas, 2 mangoes. Which fruit has the most?

visual.caption: One picture per fruit. Longest row = most (bananas, 5); shortest = least (mangoes, 2).

teachingGallery.title: Sort, count, compare
teachingGallery.note: Group alike things, count each group, then compare the rows.

standardMethod.summary: Sort into groups, count each group, draw one picture per item, then compare rows.
steps: SORT: put alike things together (all apples, all bananas).
COUNT: find how many in each group.
SHOW: draw one picture for each thing in a row.
COMPARE: longest row = most, shortest row = least.

workedExamples.problem: A chart shows 🍎🍎🍎 apples, 🍌🍌🍌🍌🍌 bananas. How many MORE bananas than apples?
Buttons sorted: red 4, blue 6, green 2. How many buttons in ALL?

commonMistakes.mistake: Choosing the group whose pictures are drawn BIGGER instead of the group with MORE pictures.
commonMistakes.fix: Count the pictures, don't look at their size. More pictures = more, even if each one is tiny.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the biggest group's count when asked for the TOTAL.
commonMistakes.fix: Total means ALL groups added together, not just the biggest one.

easy.q: Apples 3, bananas 5, mangoes 2 — which has the MOST?
The dog row has 5 pictures. How many dogs?

medium.q: Red buttons 6, blue buttons 4. How many MORE red than blue?
Cats 2, dogs 5, fish 3. How many pets in ALL?

challenge.q: Riya has 4 big star stickers and 6 tiny dot stickers. Which group has MORE stickers?
Toys sorted: cars 5, blocks 8, dolls 2. What is the total number of toys?

questions.q: In a picture chart, the group with the LONGEST row has the…
Green row has 7 pictures. How many green things?
Apples 8, oranges 3. How many more apples?
Asked for the TOTAL of red 4, blue 6, green 2 you should…

teachBackPrompt: Sort a real pile at home (buttons, toys, or crayons) into groups, count each, and draw a quick picture chart. Show a grown-up which group has the most and how many there are in all.

revisionCard.summary: Sort alike things into groups, count each, draw one picture per item. Longest row = most, shortest = least. 'How many more' = subtract the two counts; 'total' = add ALL groups. Count pictures, not their size.

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 2 (data handling — sorting & picture charts)

rememberIt.hook: Sort things into groups, then count each group
rememberIt.unpack: Put same with same, line them up, and see which group has more. The start of graphs.

studentQuestions.q: What do I do first with a pile of things?
studentQuestions.a: Sort them into groups (by colour, shape...), then count each group to compare.

studentQuestions.q: How can I show my counts?
studentQuestions.a: Line the groups up, or draw one picture per item - a simple pictograph.
